title: Retinal Detachment
oneLiner: Emergent separation of the neurosensory retina from the underlying RPE; most common in myopic, elderly, or post-cataract patients — causes sudden, painless vision loss.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**
A 55-year-old man with high myopia complains of "a sudden shower of floaters" followed by a "loud flash of light in my left eye" that he noticed while reading. Over the next few hours, he reports "a dark curtain coming up from the bottom" that has now reached the centre of his vision. He has no eye pain, no headache, and no red eye. On examination, you see a visible retinal tear at 10 o'clock with a shallow bullous detachment involving the macula.

**Dissect the vignette — pattern clues and what they tell you:**

| Clue | What it tells you |
|------|-------------------|
| High myopia / over 50 | Increased risk of posterior vitreous detachment (PVD) → rhegmatogenous RD |
| Sudden floaters, then a flash | Acute PVD with vitreous traction on retina — signs of a tear |
| "Dark curtain" spreading | Retina detaching; the field defect corresponds to the detachment location (inferior field = superior detachment) |
| No pain or red eye | Painless — rules out inflammatory causes (uveitis, endophthalmitis) |
| Central vision loss with macula involvement | Urgent — macula-on vs macula-off changes surgical timing and prognosis |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Chronic or silent RD:** Presents with slowly progressive visual field loss (patient may not notice until macular involvement). No flashes or floaters. Often missed in children or cognitively impaired patients.
- **Exudative RD:** No tear, no PVD; the retina is shifted by subretinal fluid from inflammation or a choroidal mass. May have pain, uveitis, or systemic signs.
- **Tractional RD:** In a diabetic with proliferative retinopathy — gradual, often bilateral, associated with vitreous hemorrhage. No sudden onset; traction bands are visible on exam.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**
The retina is the light-sensitive inner lining of the eye. When it peels away from the back of the eye, it stops receiving oxygen and nutrients from the underlying layer (the retinal pigment epithelium, RPE). This separation — a retinal detachment — starves the photoreceptors, leading to vision loss that can become permanent within days, especially if the central macula detaches.

**Why this matters clinically – frequency, stakes, pitfalls:**
- Incidence: ~1 in 10,000 per year; lifetime risk in high myopia >1 in 20.
- Stakes: Untreated, almost all rhegmatogenous RDs progress to total irreversible vision loss. The window for good visual recovery is typically 72 hours if macula is off.
- Common pitfalls: Misdiagnosing as migraine aura (flashes), benign floaters (only if small and settling), or posterior vitreous detachment alone (no curtain). Delayed recognition in children (white pupil) leads to advanced disease. Low threshold for ophthalmology referral saves sight.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story of rhegmatogenous RD (most common type):**

1. **Posterior vitreous detachment (PVD):** As the eye ages, the vitreous gel shrinks and separates from the retina. This is normal, but if it is too adherent, it can pull a hole or tear in the retina.

2. **Retinal tear formation:** The vitreous tug at the time of PVD creates a full-thickness defect. This explains the **flash** (mechanical traction) and **sudden floaters** (blood or pigment cells released into the vitreous).

3. **Fluid entry:** Liquefied vitreous seeps through the tear and pools between the neurosensory retina and RPE. This dissecting fluid lifts the retina off — the **curtain** corresponds to the area of detachment.

4. **Spread:** Fluid follows gravity; thus, a superior tear gives an inferior field defect (and vice versa). The macula detaches last in many cases, by which time central vision is lost.

**Why each key sign happens (mechanism → sign):**

| Mechanism | Sign |
|-----------|------|
| Vitreous traction on retina | Flash of light (photopsia) |
| Tearing of retinal vessel or RPE cell release | Floater (often described as "swarm of gnats" or "spider web") |
| Subretinal fluid accumulation | Curtain / shadow in visual field |
| Macula detachment | Loss of central and colour vision |
| No inflammation or infection | No pain, no red eye |
| Chronic detachment with photoreceptor death | "Cat's eye" reflex (white, reflective pupil) in children |

**Exudative RD:** Subretinal fluid from inflammatory cells (e.g., uveitis), neovascular membranes (e.g., wet AMD), or choroidal tumors (e.g., melanoma). No tear is present. The retina is shifted but intact.

**Tractional RD:** Fibrovascular membranes (e.g., proliferative diabetic retinopathy) pull the retina away. Often combined with vitreous hemorrhage.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
| Category | Specific cause | Stem clue |
|----------|---------------|-----------|
| **Rhegmatogenous** (most common) | Posterior vitreous detachment (PVD) | "Flash, floaters, then curtain" |
| | High myopia (>-6D) | Young adult or middle-aged with thick glasses |
| | Aphakia / pseudophakia (post-cataract surgery) | "Recent cataract operation, now vision loss" |
| | Trauma (blunt or penetrating) | "Hit in the eye with ball/fist" |
| | Lattice degeneration (thinned retina) | Incidentally found in myope; PVD triggers tear |
| **Tractional** | Proliferative diabetic retinopathy | "Gradual vision loss in a diabetic with prior laser" |
| | Retinopathy of prematurity (ROP) | Premature infant, bilateral white vitreous |
| | Proliferative vitreoretinopathy (PVR) | Previous RD repair, now recurrence |
| **Exudative** | Uveitis (e.g., Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada) | "Bilateral pain, red eye, and serous RD" |
| | Central serous chorioretinopathy | "Central blur, micropsia in young stressed male" |
| | Choroidal melanoma / metastasis | "Elevated mass with shifting subretinal fluid" |
| | Coats disease | "Child with leukocoria and telangiectatic vessels" |
| **Idiopathic** | No identifiable cause — still rhegmatogenous | Classic presentation in otherwise healthy adult |

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical diagnosis — no scoring system. The gold standard is **indirect ophthalmoscopy with scleral depression** (performed by an ophthalmologist). However, you can initiate the workup.

**Tests for the junior doctor:**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Slit-lamp exam + dilated funduscopy** | Retinal tear, detachment, vitreous cells | Confirm diagnosis in cooperative patient | "Elevated retina with rippling blood vessels" = RD. Pigment cells in vitreous ("tobacco dust") are highly suggestive of a tear |
| **B-scan ultrasound** (if media opaque) | Whole retina contour, subretinal fluid | RD cannot be directly seen (e.g., dense cataract, vitreous hemorrhage) | "A mobile, V-shaped or dome-shaped membrane attached at the optic disc" = total RD |
| **Gonioscopy** (by ophthalmologist) | Angle assessment (if trauma) | Rule out angle recession causing chronic IOP rise | Not for initial diagnosis but for surgical planning |
| **Intraocular pressure (IOP)** | Usually low (hypotony) | Low IOP occurs because of increased aqueous outflow through the tear | IOP <10 mmHg is common; if high, consider exudative cause (uveitis) or coexisting glaucoma |

**Pattern-recognition: what single finding confirms / rules out?**
- **Confirms:** Elevated retina seen ophthalmoscopically with a horseshoe tear.
- **Rules out:** A normal dilated fundus exam with clear vitreous — though a shallow, subacute RD in the periphery can be missed without scleral depression.
- **B-scan sign:** "V-shaped membrane with attached optic disc" is 100% specific for RD.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**  
- **Positioning:** In **macula-on** RD (central vision still intact), place the patient so the tear is in the most dependent position — i.e., if the tear is superior, sit upright; if inferior, supine. This reduces subretinal fluid migration to the fovea.  
- **No eye patching:** Patching does not help and delays the ophthalmology exam.  
- **Urgent ophthalmology referral:** Every patient with suspected RD should be seen **within hours**, not days.  
- **Avoid straining:** Advise patient to not bear down, bend over, or lift heavy objects, as valsalva can accelerate fluid shift.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**  
- **Definitive surgical management** — the specific technique depends on the type and location:  
  - **Pneumatic retinopexy** (gas bubble + laser/cryopexy) for small, superior tears with limited detachment.  
  - **Scleral buckle** (silicone band around the eye) for large tears, especially inferior ones.  
  - **Pars plana vitrectomy** (removal of vitreous + gas or oil tamponade) for complex RDs (tractional, giant tears, or recurrent).  
- **Preoperative drops:** Cyclopentolate 1% TID (to dilate and relax ciliary muscle) + antibiotic drops (e.g., moxifloxacin QID) if a vitrectomy is planned — **no routine systemic antibiotics**.  
- **Pain control:** Usually minimal pain; if present, consider acetaminophen 500–1000 mg PO Q6H. Avoid NSAIDs if there is concern for intraocular bleeding.  

**Long-term:**
- **Postoperative positioning:** If gas tamponade used, patient must position face-down (for macular holes) or head-turned (for tears). Duration: 1–2 weeks.  
- **Vision recovery:** Can take weeks to months; recurrence is highest in the first year (especially if PVR develops).  
- **Lifestyle:** Avoid air travel while gas is present (gas expands at altitude, causing severe high IOP).  
- **Follow-up:** Ophthalmology at 1 day, 1 week, 1 month, then yearly to monitor the fellow eye (10–15% risk of bilateral RD after first event).

**What NOT to do — common pitfalls:**
- ❌ **Do not diagnose "vitreous hemorrhage" without ruling out RD** — always dilate and examine the retina.  
- ❌ **Do not prescribe steroid drops** for presumed uveitis — can worsen a subclinical infection or cause glaucoma.  
- ❌ **Do not patch the eye** — it delays diagnosis and can create a dark environment that masks motion.  
- ❌ **Do not assume a macular hole is benign** — it may be a stage of RD.  
- ❌ **Do not use topical anesthetics** for pain — painless RD; if painful, think of other causes (endophthalmitis, acute glaucoma).

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Retinal Detachment (Rhegmatogenous) | Posterior Vitreous Detachment (PVD) | Vitreous Hemorrhage (without RD) | Central Serous Chorioretinopathy | Acute Primary Angle Closure Glaucoma |
|---------|--------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|----------------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| **Pain** | None | None | None | None | Severe, deep pain + headache |
| **Onset** | Sudden (hours) | Sudden (seconds) | Sudden (seconds to minutes) | Gradual (days) | Acute (minutes to hours) |
| **Visual symptoms** | Flashes + floaters → curtain | Flashes + floaters, no curtain | Floaters only (blobs, streaks), no curtain | Central blur, micropsia, metamorphopsia | Haloes around lights, blurred vision, red eye |
| **Exam key finding** | Elevated retina, tear, "tobacco dust" in vitreous | Weiss ring (glial ring over disc), no tear or subretinal fluid | Red blood cells in vitreous, fundus hazy | Serous detachment with small leak (on OCT), no tear | Conjunctival injection, cloudy cornea, shallow anterior chamber, iris bombé |
| **IOP** | Low (often <10) | Normal | Normal | Normal | High (>40 mmHg) |
| **Red reflex** | Gray/yellow (absent if large detachment) | Normal | Red reflex dull if dense hemorrhage | Normal or slightly blurred | Dull with corneal edema |
| **Key differentiator** | "Curtain" consistent with a retinal break on B-scan | Weiss ring, no subretinal fluid | Fresh blood obscures view; B-scan shows normal retina | OCT shows subretinal fluid under macula with no tear | Shallow AC, fixed mid-dilated pupil |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Permanent vision loss:**  
- **Macula-off RD:** If the fovea detaches, photoreceptor death begins within 24–48 hours. After 7 days, less than 50% regain functional central vision (>20/60). After 1 month, visual gain is minimal.  
- **How it presents:** Patient may not notice central loss until they cover the good eye. On exam, the macula appears pale, elevated, with a cherry‑red spot (unlike central retinal artery occlusion where the macula is opalescent).  

**Proliferative vitreoretinopathy (PVR):**  
- The retina’s response to chronic detachment — glial cells migrate, form membranes that contract and redetach the retina.  
- **Presentation:** Post‑surgical recurrence of RD, often within 3–6 months. Exam may show star folds, fixed retinal folds.  
- **What to do:** Repeat vitrectomy with membrane peeling, silicone oil tamponade, and use of perfluorocarbons. Prognosis worsens with each recurrence.  

**Hypotony maculopathy:**  
- Chronic low IOP (<6 mmHg) after RD surgery (often after scleral buckle). Leads to choroidal folds, macular edema, and vision loss.  
- **Signs:** Low IOP, choroidal detachments, retinal folds.  
- **Management:** Restore IOP by releasing the buckle or adding gas/ oil tamponade.  

**Sympathetic ophthalmia:**  
- Rare, bilateral granulomatous panuveitis after trauma or penetrating eye injury (including after multiple RD surgeries).  
- **Late presentation:** Weeks to months after surgery, blurred vision, photophobia in the uninjured eye.  
- **What to do:** Systemic corticosteroids and immunosuppression; emergency referral.  

**Late‑presentation pattern in children:**  
- **Leukocoria (white pupil reflex):** A child with chronic RD may show a "cat’s eye" appearance. This is a red flag for retinoblastoma, Coats disease, or ROP.  
- **What to do:** Urgent ophthalmic B‑scan, CT/MRI to rule out tumour, then consider RD repair if safe.  

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

| Mnemonic | Meaning | Applied |
|----------|---------|---------|
| **RRR** (Risk factors for rhegmatogenous RD) | **R** – Retinal break (tear) from **R** – PVD, **R** – Myopia, **R** – cataract surgery, **R** – trauma | In any patient with flashes/floaters, check these risk factors. |
| **SAD** (Types of RD) | **S** – Serous (exudative), **A** – Aphakic/Pseudophakic, **D** – Diabetic (tractional) | Remember non‑rhegmatogenous causes. |
| **PAINLESS** (Painless vision loss differentials) | **P** – PVD, **A** – AMD, **I** – Ischemic optic neuropathy, **N** – Neovascular (CNV), **L** – Lens dislocation, **E** – Epiretinal membrane, **S** – Stroke, **S** – Subretinal hemorrhage | RD fits here (painless). |

**7 high-yield clinical pearls:**

1. **"Tobacco dust"** (pigmented vitreous cells) in a patient with flashes and floaters is nearly pathognomonic for a retinal tear — refer immediately even if you see no detachment.
2. **Curtain that moves:** If the curtain starts inferior and shifts with head position, it is likely a **superior tear** with shifting subretinal fluid.
3. **Always dilate both eyes:** The fellow eye may have lattice degeneration or a silent tear. Bilaterality changes management.
4. **Check IOP and compare:** Low IOP in an eye with vision loss is RD until proven otherwise.
5. **B‑scan is your friend:** When you cannot see the retina (hyphema, cataract, dense vitreous hemorrhage), a B‑scan can differentiate RD from vitreous hemorrhage (no membrane moving with eye movement in pure hemorrhage).
6. **Children with white pupil:** Always think of RD (related to ROP, Coats, or trauma) before assuming cataract. A B‑scan is mandatory.
7. **Do not be reassured by normal IOP:** RD can have normal IOP if the tear is small and not draining much fluid. Always examine the retina.

**Common exam / ward mistakes:**

- ✘ Calling a vitreous hemorrhage "benign" — 20% of vitreous hemorrhages have an underlying retinal tear.
- ✘ Patching the eye and sending the patient home to "rest" — this kills time, allows macula detachment.
- ✘ Mistaking a shallow RD for a retinal scar or old detachment — new RDs are mobile, have subretinal fluid, and the retina looks "wrinkled."
- ✘ Not asking about **trauma history** — especially in young men, a seemingly minor hit can cause RD.
- ✘ Ordering a CT brain for "sudden vision loss" before dilating the eyes — delay dangerous.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call your senior / ophthalmology registrar:**

- **Any suspicion of acute retinal detachment** — even in the absence of classic curtain (e.g., unexplained sudden vision loss in a high‑risk patient).
- **Macula‑on RD** (patient still has central vision) — time is critical; loss of central vision can occur within hours.
- **Bilateral RD** or **RD in a child** — often complex, may be associated with systemic disease.
- **Post‑traumatic RD** — rule out open globe, foreign body, orbital fracture.
- **Post‑cataract surgery RD** — requires specialist vitreoretinal surgeon.

**Specific thresholds for escalation (adapted for ophthalmology)**  

| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| Vision (Snellen) | Drop from 6/6 to CF or worse in one eye | Urgent same‑day ophthalmology review |
| IOP | <6 mmHg or >40 mmHg | Sudden hypotony or acute glaucoma – call senior |
| Pupil | RAPD present (relative afferent pupillary defect) | Indicates optic nerve or severe retinal dysfunction – urgent |
| **NEWS2** (if systemic deterioration) | Score ≥5 or single parameter ≥3 | Medical emergency – may need ICU if sepsis/endophthalmitis |
| **Lactate** (if any concern for infection) | >2 mmol/L in a post‑op RD patient | Suspect endophthalmitis – immediate IV antibiotics and vitreous tap |
| **GCS** (if trauma related) | ≤13 or falling | CT head and neurosurgical consult – RD may be due to globe rupture |
| **C‑spine** (if polytrauma) | Concern for injury | Immobilize before eye exam |

**Final thought for the junior doctor:**  
Never send a patient home with "flashes and floaters" without a dilated fundus exam. The single most cost‑effective, sight‑saving step you can take is to **look at the retina**. When in doubt, call ophthalmology. Your ability to recognise the pattern — painless field loss, acute onset, no red eye — is what distinguishes a good clinician from a missed case.

rawMarkdown:
## Retinal Detachment

**One-liner:** Emergent separation of the neurosensory retina from the underlying RPE; most common in myopic, elderly, or post-cataract patients — causes sudden, painless vision loss.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**
A 55-year-old man with high myopia complains of "a sudden shower of floaters" followed by a "loud flash of light in my left eye" that he noticed while reading. Over the next few hours, he reports "a dark curtain coming up from the bottom" that has now reached the centre of his vision. He has no eye pain, no headache, and no red eye. On examination, you see a visible retinal tear at 10 o'clock with a shallow bullous detachment involving the macula.

**Dissect the vignette — pattern clues and what they tell you:**

| Clue | What it tells you |
|------|-------------------|
| High myopia / over 50 | Increased risk of posterior vitreous detachment (PVD) → rhegmatogenous RD |
| Sudden floaters, then a flash | Acute PVD with vitreous traction on retina — signs of a tear |
| "Dark curtain" spreading | Retina detaching; the field defect corresponds to the detachment location (inferior field = superior detachment) |
| No pain or red eye | Painless — rules out inflammatory causes (uveitis, endophthalmitis) |
| Central vision loss with macula involvement | Urgent — macula-on vs macula-off changes surgical timing and prognosis |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Chronic or silent RD:** Presents with slowly progressive visual field loss (patient may not notice until macular involvement). No flashes or floaters. Often missed in children or cognitively impaired patients.
- **Exudative RD:** No tear, no PVD; the retina is shifted by subretinal fluid from inflammation or a choroidal mass. May have pain, uveitis, or systemic signs.
- **Tractional RD:** In a diabetic with proliferative retinopathy — gradual, often bilateral, associated with vitreous hemorrhage. No sudden onset; traction bands are visible on exam.

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**
The retina is the light-sensitive inner lining of the eye. When it peels away from the back of the eye, it stops receiving oxygen and nutrients from the underlying layer (the retinal pigment epithelium, RPE). This separation — a retinal detachment — starves the photoreceptors, leading to vision loss that can become permanent within days, especially if the central macula detaches.

**Why this matters clinically – frequency, stakes, pitfalls:**
- Incidence: ~1 in 10,000 per year; lifetime risk in high myopia >1 in 20.
- Stakes: Untreated, almost all rhegmatogenous RDs progress to total irreversible vision loss. The window for good visual recovery is typically 72 hours if macula is off.
- Common pitfalls: Misdiagnosing as migraine aura (flashes), benign floaters (only if small and settling), or posterior vitreous detachment alone (no curtain). Delayed recognition in children (white pupil) leads to advanced disease. Low threshold for ophthalmology referral saves sight.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story of rhegmatogenous RD (most common type):**

1. **Posterior vitreous detachment (PVD):** As the eye ages, the vitreous gel shrinks and separates from the retina. This is normal, but if it is too adherent, it can pull a hole or tear in the retina.

2. **Retinal tear formation:** The vitreous tug at the time of PVD creates a full-thickness defect. This explains the **flash** (mechanical traction) and **sudden floaters** (blood or pigment cells released into the vitreous).

3. **Fluid entry:** Liquefied vitreous seeps through the tear and pools between the neurosensory retina and RPE. This dissecting fluid lifts the retina off — the **curtain** corresponds to the area of detachment.

4. **Spread:** Fluid follows gravity; thus, a superior tear gives an inferior field defect (and vice versa). The macula detaches last in many cases, by which time central vision is lost.

**Why each key sign happens (mechanism → sign):**

| Mechanism | Sign |
|-----------|------|
| Vitreous traction on retina | Flash of light (photopsia) |
| Tearing of retinal vessel or RPE cell release | Floater (often described as "swarm of gnats" or "spider web") |
| Subretinal fluid accumulation | Curtain / shadow in visual field |
| Macula detachment | Loss of central and colour vision |
| No inflammation or infection | No pain, no red eye |
| Chronic detachment with photoreceptor death | "Cat's eye" reflex (white, reflective pupil) in children |

**Exudative RD:** Subretinal fluid from inflammatory cells (e.g., uveitis), neovascular membranes (e.g., wet AMD), or choroidal tumors (e.g., melanoma). No tear is present. The retina is shifted but intact.

**Tractional RD:** Fibrovascular membranes (e.g., proliferative diabetic retinopathy) pull the retina away. Often combined with vitreous hemorrhage.

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

| Category | Specific cause | Stem clue |
|----------|---------------|-----------|
| **Rhegmatogenous** (most common) | Posterior vitreous detachment (PVD) | "Flash, floaters, then curtain" |
| | High myopia (>-6D) | Young adult or middle-aged with thick glasses |
| | Aphakia / pseudophakia (post-cataract surgery) | "Recent cataract operation, now vision loss" |
| | Trauma (blunt or penetrating) | "Hit in the eye with ball/fist" |
| | Lattice degeneration (thinned retina) | Incidentally found in myope; PVD triggers tear |
| **Tractional** | Proliferative diabetic retinopathy | "Gradual vision loss in a diabetic with prior laser" |
| | Retinopathy of prematurity (ROP) | Premature infant, bilateral white vitreous |
| | Proliferative vitreoretinopathy (PVR) | Previous RD repair, now recurrence |
| **Exudative** | Uveitis (e.g., Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada) | "Bilateral pain, red eye, and serous RD" |
| | Central serous chorioretinopathy | "Central blur, micropsia in young stressed male" |
| | Choroidal melanoma / metastasis | "Elevated mass with shifting subretinal fluid" |
| | Coats disease | "Child with leukocoria and telangiectatic vessels" |
| **Idiopathic** | No identifiable cause — still rhegmatogenous | Classic presentation in otherwise healthy adult |

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical diagnosis — no scoring system. The gold standard is **indirect ophthalmoscopy with scleral depression** (performed by an ophthalmologist). However, you can initiate the workup.

**Tests for the junior doctor:**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Slit-lamp exam + dilated funduscopy** | Retinal tear, detachment, vitreous cells | Confirm diagnosis in cooperative patient | "Elevated retina with rippling blood vessels" = RD. Pigment cells in vitreous ("tobacco dust") are highly suggestive of a tear |
| **B-scan ultrasound** (if media opaque) | Whole retina contour, subretinal fluid | RD cannot be directly seen (e.g., dense cataract, vitreous hemorrhage) | "A mobile, V-shaped or dome-shaped membrane attached at the optic disc" = total RD |
| **Gonioscopy** (by ophthalmologist) | Angle assessment (if trauma) | Rule out angle recession causing chronic IOP rise | Not for initial diagnosis but for surgical planning |
| **Intraocular pressure (IOP)** | Usually low (hypotony) | Low IOP occurs because of increased aqueous outflow through the tear | IOP <10 mmHg is common; if high, consider exudative cause (uveitis) or coexisting glaucoma |

**Pattern-recognition: what single finding confirms / rules out?**
- **Confirms:** Elevated retina seen ophthalmoscopically with a horseshoe tear.
- **Rules out:** A normal dilated fundus exam with clear vitreous — though a shallow, subacute RD in the periphery can be missed without scleral depression.
- **B-scan sign:** "V-shaped membrane with attached optic disc" is 100% specific for RD.

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour):**  
- **Positioning:** In **macula-on** RD (central vision still intact), place the patient so the tear is in the most dependent position — i.e., if the tear is superior, sit upright; if inferior, supine. This reduces subretinal fluid migration to the fovea.  
- **No eye patching:** Patching does not help and delays the ophthalmology exam.  
- **Urgent ophthalmology referral:** Every patient with suspected RD should be seen **within hours**, not days.  
- **Avoid straining:** Advise patient to not bear down, bend over, or lift heavy objects, as valsalva can accelerate fluid shift.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**  
- **Definitive surgical management** — the specific technique depends on the type and location:  
  - **Pneumatic retinopexy** (gas bubble + laser/cryopexy) for small, superior tears with limited detachment.  
  - **Scleral buckle** (silicone band around the eye) for large tears, especially inferior ones.  
  - **Pars plana vitrectomy** (removal of vitreous + gas or oil tamponade) for complex RDs (tractional, giant tears, or recurrent).  
- **Preoperative drops:** Cyclopentolate 1% TID (to dilate and relax ciliary muscle) + antibiotic drops (e.g., moxifloxacin QID) if a vitrectomy is planned — **no routine systemic antibiotics**.  
- **Pain control:** Usually minimal pain; if present, consider acetaminophen 500–1000 mg PO Q6H. Avoid NSAIDs if there is concern for intraocular bleeding.  

**Long-term:**
- **Postoperative positioning:** If gas tamponade used, patient must position face-down (for macular holes) or head-turned (for tears). Duration: 1–2 weeks.  
- **Vision recovery:** Can take weeks to months; recurrence is highest in the first year (especially if PVR develops).  
- **Lifestyle:** Avoid air travel while gas is present (gas expands at altitude, causing severe high IOP).  
- **Follow-up:** Ophthalmology at 1 day, 1 week, 1 month, then yearly to monitor the fellow eye (10–15% risk of bilateral RD after first event).

**What NOT to do — common pitfalls:**
- ❌ **Do not diagnose "vitreous hemorrhage" without ruling out RD** — always dilate and examine the retina.  
- ❌ **Do not prescribe steroid drops** for presumed uveitis — can worsen a subclinical infection or cause glaucoma.  
- ❌ **Do not patch the eye** — it delays diagnosis and can create a dark environment that masks motion.  
- ❌ **Do not assume a macular hole is benign** — it may be a stage of RD.  
- ❌ **Do not use topical anesthetics** for pain — painless RD; if painful, think of other causes (endophthalmitis, acute glaucoma).

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Retinal Detachment (Rhegmatogenous) | Posterior Vitreous Detachment (PVD) | Vitreous Hemorrhage (without RD) | Central Serous Chorioretinopathy | Acute Primary Angle Closure Glaucoma |
|---------|--------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|----------------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| **Pain** | None | None | None | None | Severe, deep pain + headache |
| **Onset** | Sudden (hours) | Sudden (seconds) | Sudden (seconds to minutes) | Gradual (days) | Acute (minutes to hours) |
| **Visual symptoms** | Flashes + floaters → curtain | Flashes + floaters, no curtain | Floaters only (blobs, streaks), no curtain | Central blur, micropsia, metamorphopsia | Haloes around lights, blurred vision, red eye |
| **Exam key finding** | Elevated retina, tear, "tobacco dust" in vitreous | Weiss ring (glial ring over disc), no tear or subretinal fluid | Red blood cells in vitreous, fundus hazy | Serous detachment with small leak (on OCT), no tear | Conjunctival injection, cloudy cornea, shallow anterior chamber, iris bombé |
| **IOP** | Low (often <10) | Normal | Normal | Normal | High (>40 mmHg) |
| **Red reflex** | Gray/yellow (absent if large detachment) | Normal | Red reflex dull if dense hemorrhage | Normal or slightly blurred | Dull with corneal edema |
| **Key differentiator** | "Curtain" consistent with a retinal break on B-scan | Weiss ring, no subretinal fluid | Fresh blood obscures view; B-scan shows normal retina | OCT shows subretinal fluid under macula with no tear | Shallow AC, fixed mid-dilated pupil |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Permanent vision loss:**  
- **Macula-off RD:** If the fovea detaches, photoreceptor death begins within 24–48 hours. After 7 days, less than 50% regain functional central vision (>20/60). After 1 month, visual gain is minimal.  
- **How it presents:** Patient may not notice central loss until they cover the good eye. On exam, the macula appears pale, elevated, with a cherry‑red spot (unlike central retinal artery occlusion where the macula is opalescent).  

**Proliferative vitreoretinopathy (PVR):**  
- The retina’s response to chronic detachment — glial cells migrate, form membranes that contract and redetach the retina.  
- **Presentation:** Post‑surgical recurrence of RD, often within 3–6 months. Exam may show star folds, fixed retinal folds.  
- **What to do:** Repeat vitrectomy with membrane peeling, silicone oil tamponade, and use of perfluorocarbons. Prognosis worsens with each recurrence.  

**Hypotony maculopathy:**  
- Chronic low IOP (<6 mmHg) after RD surgery (often after scleral buckle). Leads to choroidal folds, macular edema, and vision loss.  
- **Signs:** Low IOP, choroidal detachments, retinal folds.  
- **Management:** Restore IOP by releasing the buckle or adding gas/ oil tamponade.  

**Sympathetic ophthalmia:**  
- Rare, bilateral granulomatous panuveitis after trauma or penetrating eye injury (including after multiple RD surgeries).  
- **Late presentation:** Weeks to months after surgery, blurred vision, photophobia in the uninjured eye.  
- **What to do:** Systemic corticosteroids and immunosuppression; emergency referral.  

**Late‑presentation pattern in children:**  
- **Leukocoria (white pupil reflex):** A child with chronic RD may show a "cat’s eye" appearance. This is a red flag for retinoblastoma, Coats disease, or ROP.  
- **What to do:** Urgent ophthalmic B‑scan, CT/MRI to rule out tumour, then consider RD repair if safe.  

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

| Mnemonic | Meaning | Applied |
|----------|---------|---------|
| **RRR** (Risk factors for rhegmatogenous RD) | **R** – Retinal break (tear) from **R** – PVD, **R** – Myopia, **R** – cataract surgery, **R** – trauma | In any patient with flashes/floaters, check these risk factors. |
| **SAD** (Types of RD) | **S** – Serous (exudative), **A** – Aphakic/Pseudophakic, **D** – Diabetic (tractional) | Remember non‑rhegmatogenous causes. |
| **PAINLESS** (Painless vision loss differentials) | **P** – PVD, **A** – AMD, **I** – Ischemic optic neuropathy, **N** – Neovascular (CNV), **L** – Lens dislocation, **E** – Epiretinal membrane, **S** – Stroke, **S** – Subretinal hemorrhage | RD fits here (painless). |

**7 high-yield clinical pearls:**

1. **"Tobacco dust"** (pigmented vitreous cells) in a patient with flashes and floaters is nearly pathognomonic for a retinal tear — refer immediately even if you see no detachment.
2. **Curtain that moves:** If the curtain starts inferior and shifts with head position, it is likely a **superior tear** with shifting subretinal fluid.
3. **Always dilate both eyes:** The fellow eye may have lattice degeneration or a silent tear. Bilaterality changes management.
4. **Check IOP and compare:** Low IOP in an eye with vision loss is RD until proven otherwise.
5. **B‑scan is your friend:** When you cannot see the retina (hyphema, cataract, dense vitreous hemorrhage), a B‑scan can differentiate RD from vitreous hemorrhage (no membrane moving with eye movement in pure hemorrhage).
6. **Children with white pupil:** Always think of RD (related to ROP, Coats, or trauma) before assuming cataract. A B‑scan is mandatory.
7. **Do not be reassured by normal IOP:** RD can have normal IOP if the tear is small and not draining much fluid. Always examine the retina.

**Common exam / ward mistakes:**

- ✘ Calling a vitreous hemorrhage "benign" — 20% of vitreous hemorrhages have an underlying retinal tear.
- ✘ Patching the eye and sending the patient home to "rest" — this kills time, allows macula detachment.
- ✘ Mistaking a shallow RD for a retinal scar or old detachment — new RDs are mobile, have subretinal fluid, and the retina looks "wrinkled."
- ✘ Not asking about **trauma history** — especially in young men, a seemingly minor hit can cause RD.
- ✘ Ordering a CT brain for "sudden vision loss" before dilating the eyes — delay dangerous.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call your senior / ophthalmology registrar:**

- **Any suspicion of acute retinal detachment** — even in the absence of classic curtain (e.g., unexplained sudden vision loss in a high‑risk patient).
- **Macula‑on RD** (patient still has central vision) — time is critical; loss of central vision can occur within hours.
- **Bilateral RD** or **RD in a child** — often complex, may be associated with systemic disease.
- **Post‑traumatic RD** — rule out open globe, foreign body, orbital fracture.
- **Post‑cataract surgery RD** — requires specialist vitreoretinal surgeon.

**Specific thresholds for escalation (adapted for ophthalmology)**  

| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| Vision (Snellen) | Drop from 6/6 to CF or worse in one eye | Urgent same‑day ophthalmology review |
| IOP | <6 mmHg or >40 mmHg | Sudden hypotony or acute glaucoma – call senior |
| Pupil | RAPD present (relative afferent pupillary defect) | Indicates optic nerve or severe retinal dysfunction – urgent |
| **NEWS2** (if systemic deterioration) | Score ≥5 or single parameter ≥3 | Medical emergency – may need ICU if sepsis/endophthalmitis |
| **Lactate** (if any concern for infection) | >2 mmol/L in a post‑op RD patient | Suspect endophthalmitis – immediate IV antibiotics and vitreous tap |
| **GCS** (if trauma related) | ≤13 or falling | CT head and neurosurgical consult – RD may be due to globe rupture |
| **C‑spine** (if polytrauma) | Concern for injury | Immobilize before eye exam |

**Final thought for the junior doctor:**  
Never send a patient home with "flashes and floaters" without a dilated fundus exam. The single most cost‑effective, sight‑saving step you can take is to **look at the retina**. When in doubt, call ophthalmology. Your ability to recognise the pattern — painless field loss, acute onset, no red eye — is what distinguishes a good clinician from a missed case.
